title: Sort stack using recursion
description: Sort a stack using recursion only

examples.explanation: Initial stack: [5, 1, 4, 2, 3]. sort_stack called: pop 3, stack becomes [5, 1, 4, 2]; recursively sort [5, 1, 4, 2]. sort_stack: pop 2, stack becomes [5, 1, 4]; recursively sort [5, 1, 4]. sort_stack: pop 4, stack becomes [5, 1]; recursively sort [5, 1]. sort_stack: pop 1, stack becomes [5]; recursively sort [5]. sort_stack: pop 5, stack becomes []; recursively sort [] (base case). Now insert back: insert_sorted([], 5) pushes 5 → [5]. insert_sorted([5], 1): since 5 > 1, pop 5, insert_sorted([], 1) pushes 1 → [1], then push back 5 → [1, 5]. insert_sorted([1, 5], 4): since 5 > 4, pop 5, insert_sorted([1], 4): since 1 <= 4, push 4 → [1, 4], then push back 5 → [1, 4, 5]. insert_sorted([1, 4, 5], 2): since 5 > 2, pop 5; since 4 > 2, pop 4; since 1 <= 2, push 2 → [1, 2]; push back 4 → [1, 2, 4]; push back 5 → [1, 2, 4, 5]. insert_sorted([1, 2, 4, 5], 3): since 5 > 3, pop 5; since 4 > 3, pop 4; since 2 <= 3, push 3 → [1, 2, 3]; push back 4 → [1, 2, 3, 4]; push back 5 → [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. Final sorted stack: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Recursive Sort with Insertion
approaches.summary: Use recursion to pop all elements from the stack, then recursively insert each element back into the stack in sorted order by comparing and shifting elements as needed. This mimics an insertion sort approach adapted for a stack using recursion.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Stack Using Recursive Insertion
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to sort a stack using recursion only, mimicking insertion sort adapted for stacks.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires sorting a stack using only recursion, without auxiliary data structures. The approach uses two recursive functions: one to sort the stack and another to insert elements in sorted order. The intuition is to treat the stack as a collection where we can only access the top element. We recursively pop all elements, then insert each back in sorted order by comparing with the current top and shifting elements as needed. This mimics insertion sort: we maintain that the stack below the current recursion level is always sorted. The algorithm works by first defining an insert_sorted function that takes an element and a stack, recursively popping elements larger than the given element to find the correct position, pushing the element, then pushing back the larger elements. The sort_stack function recursively pops all elements, sorts the remaining stack, then inserts each popped element back using insert_sorted. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested recursion for each element, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion call stack. This connects to L1 as it combines the key sub-routine with the overall sorting logic. Common mistakes include not handling empty stacks in base cases, incorrect comparison logic in insert_sorted, or infinite recursion from missing base cases. Real code pattern: def insert_sorted(stack, element): if not stack or stack[-1] <= element: stack.append(element) else: temp = stack.pop(); insert_sorted(stack, element); stack.append(temp). def sort_stack(stack): if stack: temp = stack.pop(); sort_stack(stack); insert_sorted(stack, temp).

examples.trace: Initial stack: [5, 1, 4, 2, 3]. sort_stack called: pop 3, stack becomes [5, 1, 4, 2]; recursively sort [5, 1, 4, 2]. sort_stack: pop 2, stack becomes [5, 1, 4]; recursively sort [5, 1, 4]. sort_stack: pop 4, stack becomes [5, 1]; recursively sort [5, 1]. sort_stack: pop 1, stack becomes [5]; recursively sort [5]. sort_stack: pop 5, stack becomes []; recursively sort [] (base case). Now insert back: insert_sorted([], 5) pushes 5 → [5]. insert_sorted([5], 1): since 5 > 1, pop 5, insert_sorted([], 1) pushes 1 → [1], then push back 5 → [1, 5]. insert_sorted([1, 5], 4): since 5 > 4, pop 5, insert_sorted([1], 4): since 1 <= 4, push 4 → [1, 4], then push back 5 → [1, 4, 5]. insert_sorted([1, 4, 5], 2): since 5 > 2, pop 5; since 4 > 2, pop 4; since 1 <= 2, push 2 → [1, 2]; push back 4 → [1, 2, 4]; push back 5 → [1, 2, 4, 5]. insert_sorted([1, 2, 4, 5], 3): since 5 > 3, pop 5; since 4 > 3, pop 4; since 2 <= 3, push 3 → [1, 2, 3]; push back 4 → [1, 2, 3, 4]; push back 5 → [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. Final sorted stack: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert Element into Sorted Stack Recursively
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive function to insert an element into a stack that is already sorted in ascending order.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the sorting approach. The intuition is that if we have a stack sorted in ascending order (top is smallest), we can insert a new element while maintaining the sorted order by recursively comparing and shifting elements. The function takes a stack and an element as input. Base case: if the stack is empty or the top element is less than or equal to the element to insert, we push the element onto the stack. Recursive case: if the top element is greater than the element to insert, we pop the top element, recursively insert the element into the remaining stack, then push the popped element back. This works because we temporarily remove larger elements to find the correct insertion point, then restore them above the inserted element. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case as we might traverse the entire stack, and space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L0 as it is used within sort_stack to insert each popped element. Common mistakes include incorrect comparison (using < instead of <= for stable insertion), not handling empty stack in base case, or forgetting to push back popped elements. Real code pattern: def insert_sorted(stack, element): if not stack or stack[-1] <= element: stack.append(element) else: temp = stack.pop(); insert_sorted(stack, element); stack.append(temp).

examples.trace: Initial stack: [1, 3, 5], element = 4. insert_sorted called: top is 5, 5 > 4, so pop 5 → stack becomes [1, 3]; temp = 5. Recursively insert_sorted([1, 3], 4): top is 3, 3 <= 4, so push 4 → stack becomes [1, 3, 4]. Then push back temp 5 → stack becomes [1, 3, 4, 5]. Final: [1, 3, 4, 5].

Initial stack: [2, 4, 6], element = 1. insert_sorted: top is 6, 6 > 1, pop 6 → [2, 4]; temp = 6. insert_sorted([2, 4], 1): top is 4, 4 > 1, pop 4 → [2]; temp2 = 4. insert_sorted([2], 1): top is 2, 2 > 1, pop 2 → []; temp3 = 2. insert_sorted([], 1): empty stack, push 1 → [1]. Push back temp3 2 → [1, 2]. Push back temp2 4 → [1, 2, 4]. Push back temp 6 → [1, 2, 4, 6]. Final: [1, 2, 4, 6].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursively Maintain Sorted Order by Insertion
ladders.desc: Understand how recursion can be used to keep a stack sorted by inserting elements in correct positions.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central insight: using recursion to maintain a sorted stack by inserting elements one by one. The intuition is that if we have a sorted stack, we can insert a new element while preserving order by comparing it with the top element. If the new element is larger or equal to the top, it can be pushed directly; if smaller, we recursively pop larger elements, insert the new element at the correct depth, then push the larger elements back. This mimics the insertion step of insertion sort, adapted for a stack's LIFO structure. The key is that recursion allows us to 'drill down' through the stack to find the insertion point without using loops or extra data structures. The algorithm: function insert(sorted_stack, element): if sorted_stack is empty or top <= element: push element; else: pop top; insert(sorted_stack, element); push popped top. Time complexity is O(n) per insertion, and space is O(n) for recursion. This connects to L1 as it explains the logic behind the insert_sorted function. Common mistakes include not understanding that the stack must be sorted initially for this to work, or misapplying the comparison logic. Real code pattern: same as L1 but focusing on conceptual understanding.

examples.trace: Start: [2, 5, 9], insert 7. Compare 9 and 7: 9 > 7, so pop 9 → [2, 5]; hold 9. Recursively insert 7 into [2, 5]: compare 5 and 7: 5 <= 7, so push 7 → [2, 5, 7]. Then push back 9 → [2, 5, 7, 9]. Sorted order maintained.

Start: [1, 3, 6], insert 0. Compare 6 and 0: 6 > 0, pop 6 → [1, 3]; hold 6. Insert 0 into [1, 3]: compare 3 and 0: 3 > 0, pop 3 → [1]; hold 3. Insert 0 into [1]: compare 1 and 0: 1 > 0, pop 1 → []; hold 1. Insert 0 into []: empty, push 0 → [0]. Push back 1 → [0, 1]. Push back 3 → [0, 1, 3]. Push back 6 → [0, 1, 3, 6]. Sorted.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Stack Traversal and Element Removal
ladders.desc: Learn to recursively pop elements from a stack and store them in the call stack for later processing.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves using recursion to traverse and manipulate a stack by popping elements. The intuition is that recursion allows us to access elements in reverse order: we pop an element, recursively process the remaining stack, then handle the popped element. This is key because stacks only allow access to the top element; recursion uses the call stack to temporarily store popped elements. The algorithm: function process_stack(stack): if stack is not empty: temp = pop(stack); process_stack(stack); // process remaining stack do_something_with(temp); // e.g., insert back. This ensures we process elements from bottom to top when combined with insertion. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing all elements, and space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L2 as it provides the mechanism to access all elements for sorting. Common mistakes include not having a base case to stop recursion, or misunderstanding the order of operations (popping before recursive call). Real code pattern: def traverse(stack): if stack: element = stack.pop(); traverse(stack); print(element) // prints in original order.

examples.trace: Initial stack: [1, 2, 3]. traverse called: pop 3 → stack becomes [1, 2]; temp = 3. Recursively traverse([1, 2]): pop 2 → [1]; temp2 = 2. traverse([1]): pop 1 → []; temp3 = 1. traverse([]): base case, return. Now process temps: print temp3 = 1; then print temp2 = 2; then print temp = 3. Output: 1, 2, 3 (original order).

Start: [5, 10, 15]. pop_all called: pop 15 → [5, 10]; store 15 in call stack. pop_all([5, 10]): pop 10 → [5]; store 10. pop_all([5]): pop 5 → []; store 5. pop_all([]): base case. After recursion, stack is empty. Elements 15, 10, 5 are stored in recursion calls.

testCases.input: stack = [4, 2, 6], traverse and collect in list

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop with Recursion Base Cases
ladders.desc: Master the fundamental stack operations and how to use recursion with base cases to manipulate stacks.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operations of pushing and popping elements from a stack, and how to use recursion with proper base cases to avoid infinite loops. The intuition is that stacks are LIFO data structures; pushing adds to the top, popping removes from the top. Recursion requires a base case to stop the recursive calls, typically when the stack is empty or a condition is met. For stack manipulation, we often use recursion to process elements by popping until empty (base case), then handling elements on the way back. The algorithm: function recursive_op(stack): if stack is empty: return // base case; else: element = stack.pop(); recursive_op(stack); // process remaining stack.push(element) // or other operation. Time complexity depends on operations, but basic push/pop are O(1); recursion adds O(n) time and space. This connects to L3 as it provides the foundation for traversing stacks recursively. Common mistakes include forgetting base cases leading to stack overflow, or popping without storing elements. Real code pattern: def process(stack): if not stack: return; top = stack.pop(); process(stack); stack.append(top) // restores stack.

examples.input: Stack = [1, 2, 3], use recursion to double each element
examples.trace: Start: [1, 2, 3]. double_stack called: not empty, pop 3 → [1, 2]; temp = 3. double_stack([1, 2]): pop 2 → [1]; temp2 = 2. double_stack([1]): pop 1 → []; temp3 = 1. double_stack([]): base case, return. Now push back doubled: push temp3*2 = 2 → [2]; push temp2*2 = 4 → [2, 4]; push temp*2 = 6 → [2, 4, 6]. Final: [2, 4, 6].
Start: [5, 10]. clear_stack called: not empty, pop 10 → [5]; store 10. clear_stack([5]): pop 5 → []; store 5. clear_stack([]): base case, return. Stack is empty; elements 10 and 5 are in call stack.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Stacks and Recursion Basics
ladders.desc: Learn the fundamental concepts of stack data structure and how recursion works with simple examples.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite level. A stack is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) data structure where elements are added (pushed) and removed (popped) from the top. Think of it like a stack of plates: you add or remove from the top only. Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem. It requires a base case to stop the calls. For beginners, understand that recursion uses a call stack to manage function calls; each recursive call adds a frame to this stack. The intuition for combining stacks and recursion is that recursion can mimic stack operations by using the call stack to store state. Simple example: function factorial(n): if n == 0: return 1 (base case); else: return n * factorial(n-1). For stacks, we might use recursion to process elements by popping until empty. Time complexity for basic operations is O(1) for push/pop, but recursion can add O(n) time and space. This connects to L4 as it provides the groundwork for stack manipulation with recursion. Common mistakes include not defining a base case, leading to infinite recursion, or misunderstanding LIFO order. Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append(1) // push; top = stack.pop() // pop; def recursive_print(n): if n <= 0: return; print(n); recursive_print(n-1).

examples.trace: Start with empty stack. Push 1 → stack: [1]. Push 2 → stack: [1, 2]. Pop removes top 2 → stack: [1]. Push 3 → stack: [1, 3]. Final: [1, 3].

examples.input: Recursive function to print numbers from 3 to 1
examples.trace: Call print_numbers(3): n=3, not base case (n>0), print 3. Call print_numbers(2): print 2. Call print_numbers(1): print 1. Call print_numbers(0): base case (n==0), return. Output: 3, 2, 1.

testCases.input: stack = [a, b], pop then push c

approaches.name: Approach 2: Recursive Sort with Temporary Stack
approaches.summary: Recursively pop elements from the original stack and use a temporary stack to hold sorted elements by inserting each popped element in the correct position through recursive comparisons and pushes. This method explicitly maintains a sorted auxiliary stack during the recursion.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Stack Using Recursive Sort with Temporary Stack
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to sort a stack using recursion with an auxiliary stack for sorted elements.
ladders.explanation:
This approach sorts a stack by recursively popping all elements from the original stack, then using a temporary stack to maintain sorted order as elements are reinserted. The intuition is that we can treat the sorting process as two recursive phases: first, we empty the original stack completely through recursion, storing elements in the call stack. Second, we insert each element into a temporary stack in sorted order using another recursive function. The temporary stack acts as our sorted collection, with the largest elements at the bottom. The key insight is that by recursively popping all elements, we can process them one by one in reverse order of removal, inserting each into the correct position in the temporary stack through comparisons. The algorithm works in O(n²) time because each insertion may require moving multiple elements in the temporary stack, and O(n) space for the recursion call stack and temporary stack. This connects to the overall problem by providing a complete recursive solution without explicit loops. Common mistakes include not handling empty stacks correctly in the insertion function or infinite recursion from incorrect base cases. The real pattern involves two recursive functions: one to empty the stack and another to insert elements in sorted order.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Define function insert_sorted(temp_stack, element):
   - If temp_stack is empty OR element >= top of temp_stack: push element to temp_stack and return
   - Else: pop top from temp_stack, recursively call insert_sorted with element, then push popped element back
2. Define function sort_stack(original_stack):
   - If original_stack is not empty: pop top element, recursively call sort_stack on remaining stack, then call insert_sorted(temp_stack, popped_element)
3. After sort_stack completes, transfer all elements from temp_stack back to original_stack to get ascending order (top is smallest)

Time: O(n²) worst-case when stack is reverse sorted, as each insertion may require moving all elements in temp_stack. Space: O(n) for recursion depth and temporary stack storage.

examples.trace:
Original stack: [5,1,4,2,3] (top=5)
1. sort_stack pops 5, calls sort_stack on [1,4,2,3]
2. sort_stack pops 1, calls sort_stack on [4,2,3]
3. sort_stack pops 4, calls sort_stack on [2,3]
4. sort_stack pops 2, calls sort_stack on [3]
5. sort_stack pops 3, calls sort_stack on [] (base case)
6. Now inserting back: insert_sorted(temp,3) → temp=[3]
7. insert_sorted(temp,2): 2<3, pop 3, insert_sorted(temp,2)→temp=[2], push 3→temp=[2,3]
8. insert_sorted(temp,4): 4>3, push 4→temp=[2,3,4]
9. insert_sorted(temp,1): 1<2, pop 2, insert_sorted(temp,1)→temp=[1], push 2→temp=[1,2], pop 3, insert_sorted(temp,3)→temp=[1,2,3], push 4→temp=[1,2,3,4]
10. insert_sorted(temp,5): 5>4, push 5→temp=[1,2,3,4,5]
11. Transfer temp to original: pop all→original=[1,2,3,4,5] (top=1)

Original stack: [10,-5,0,7] (top=10)
1. sort_stack pops 10, calls sort_stack on [-5,0,7]
2. sort_stack pops -5, calls sort_stack on [0,7]
3. sort_stack pops 0, calls sort_stack on [7]
4. sort_stack pops 7, calls sort_stack on []
5. insert_sorted(temp,7)→temp=[7]
6. insert_sorted(temp,0): 0<7, pop 7, insert_sorted(temp,0)→temp=[0], push 7→temp=[0,7]
7. insert_sorted(temp,-5): -5<0, pop 0, insert_sorted(temp,-5)→temp=[-5], push 0→temp=[-5,0], pop 7, insert_sorted(temp,7)→temp=[-5,0,7]
8. insert_sorted(temp,10): 10>7, push 10→temp=[-5,0,7,10]
9. Transfer to original: original=[-5,0,7,10] (top=-5)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Insert into Sorted Stack
ladders.desc: Insert an element into a temporary stack while maintaining sorted order using recursion.

examples.trace:
Start: temp=[5,3,1], element=2
1. 2 < 5, pop 5→temp=[3,1], call insert_sorted(temp,2)
2. 2 < 3, pop 3→temp=[1], call insert_sorted(temp,2)
3. 2 > 1, push 2→temp=[1,2]
4. Push back 3→temp=[1,2,3]
5. Push back 5→temp=[1,2,3,5]
Note: Actually sorted descending, so final: [5,3,2,1] after reversing trace steps.

Start: temp=[10,7], element=12
1. 12 >= 10, push 12→temp=[10,7,12]
Sorted descending: [12,10,7]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Sorted Order Through Recursive Comparisons
ladders.desc: The technique of using recursion to compare and reposition elements in a stack to keep it sorted.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic involves the recursive strategy for inserting an element into a sorted stack by comparing it with existing elements and temporarily removing larger ones to place it correctly. The intuition is that a stack sorted in descending order (largest at bottom) can be maintained by ensuring that every new element is placed such that all elements above it are smaller or equal. Recursion allows us to handle this by drilling down through the stack: when we encounter a larger element at the top, we pop it, recursively insert the new element deeper where it might fit, then restore the popped element. This works because the recursion naturally reverses the order of operations—we remove elements, place the new one, then rebuild the stack. The step-by-step process: 1. Check if element can be placed at current position (if stack empty or element >= top). 2. If yes, push and done. 3. If no, pop top, recurse to insert element lower, then push popped element back. Time complexity per operation depends on stack depth, up to O(n), and space is O(n) for recursion calls. This feeds into the key sub-routine by being the algorithmic heart of the insertion function. Common mistakes include not preserving the popped elements correctly or infinite recursion from missing base cases. The real pattern is a divide-and-conquer style where each recursive call handles a smaller subproblem (the stack without the top element).

Pseudocode illustrating the logic:
def insert_logic(stack, elem):
    if stack.empty() or elem >= stack.top():
        # Place element here
        stack.push(elem)
    else:
        # Remove larger element, insert deeper, then restore
        larger = stack.pop()
        insert_logic(stack, elem)
        stack.push(larger)

examples.trace:
Start: stack=[8,5,2], elem=6
1. 6 < 8, pop 8→stack=[5,2], call insert_logic(stack,6)
2. 6 < 5? No, 6 > 5, so push 6→stack=[5,2,6]
3. Push back 8→stack=[5,2,6,8]
Sorted descending: [8,6,5,2]

Start: stack=[4,1], elem=3
1. 3 < 4, pop 4→stack=[1], call insert_logic(stack,3)
2. 3 > 1, push 3→stack=[1,3]
3. Push back 4→stack=[1,3,4]
Sorted descending: [4,3,1]

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Stack Traversal and Element Removal
ladders.desc: Recursively pop all elements from a stack to process them in reverse order.
ladders.explanation:
This building block involves using recursion to traverse a stack by popping elements until it's empty, which allows processing elements in the reverse order of their original positions. The intuition is that recursion naturally reverses order: when we pop an element and recurse on the remaining stack, the element is processed after the recursive call returns, effectively handling elements from bottom to top. This works because each recursive call removes one element from the top, reducing the problem size, and the call stack stores the popped elements for later use. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Base case: if stack is empty, return. 2. Otherwise, pop the top element, recursively call the function on the stack, then process the popped element (e.g., insert it into a sorted stack). Time complexity is O(n) to pop all elements, and space is O(n) for the recursion call stack. This feeds into the core logic by providing the mechanism to access each element individually for insertion. Common mistakes include not storing the popped element before recursion or infinite recursion from incorrect base condition. The real pattern is a simple recursive function that reduces stack size by one each call.

Pseudocode:
def traverse_and_pop(stack):
    if stack is empty:
        return
    top = stack.pop()
    traverse_and_pop(stack)  # Recurse on smaller stack
    # Now process 'top' element (e.g., insert into sorted stack)

examples.output: Elements popped in order: 3, 1, 2 (but processed in reverse: 2, 1, 3 after recursion)
examples.trace:
Start: stack=[3,1,2]
1. Pop 3, stack=[1,2], call traverse_and_pop(stack)
2. Pop 1, stack=[2], call traverse_and_pop(stack)
3. Pop 2, stack=[], call traverse_and_pop(stack) → base case returns
4. Process 2 (e.g., insert)
5. Process 1
6. Process 3

Start: stack=[5]
1. Pop 5, stack=[], call traverse_and_pop(stack) → base case
2. Process 5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop with Recursion Base Case
ladders.desc: Fundamental stack operations and how to use them within recursive functions with proper termination conditions.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation covers the essential stack manipulations—push (add to top) and pop (remove from top)—and how to integrate them into recursive algorithms with a base case to prevent infinite recursion. The intuition is that stacks are LIFO (last-in, first-out) data structures, and recursion relies on reducing a problem to a smaller version; combining these requires careful handling to avoid errors like popping from an empty stack. The push operation adds an element to the top, and pop removes and returns the top element. In recursion, we use pop to reduce the stack size and push to rebuild it. The base case is crucial: typically when the stack is empty, we stop recursion to avoid infinite calls. This works because each recursive call processes one element via pop, and the base case ensures termination. Step-by-step: 1. Check if stack is empty (base case). 2. If not, pop top element. 3. Recurse on the remaining stack. 4. After recursion, possibly push elements back. Time and space are O(1) per operation, but overall depends on recursion depth. This feeds into the building block by providing the mechanical steps for stack traversal. Common mistakes include not checking for empty stack before pop or missing push operations after recursion. The real pattern is using stack operations within a recursive function that has a clear base case.

Pseudocode:
def recursive_stack_op(stack):
    if stack is empty:  # Base case
        return
    top = stack.pop()   # Remove element
    recursive_stack_op(stack)  # Recurse
    stack.push(top)     # Optional: push back

examples.input: Stack: [2, 4, 6], function that pops and prints in reverse
examples.trace:
Start: stack=[2,4,6]
1. Not empty, pop 2→stack=[4,6], call recursive_stack_op(stack)
2. Not empty, pop 4→stack=[6], call recursive_stack_op(stack)
3. Not empty, pop 6→stack=[], call recursive_stack_op(stack)
4. Empty, base case returns
5. Print 6 (after recursion returns)
6. Print 4
7. Print 2

examples.input: Stack: [5], function that pops and pushes back after recursion
examples.trace:
Start: stack=[5]
1. Not empty, pop 5→stack=[], call recursive_stack_op(stack)
2. Empty, base case returns
3. Push back 5→stack=[5]

testCases.input: Stack=[7,5,3], op=pop all and push back
Stack=[1,2], op=pop and not push back

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion and Stack Data Structure
ladders.desc: Introduction to recursion as a function calling itself and stacks as LIFO collections.
ladders.explanation:
This foundation covers the prerequisite concepts: recursion and stacks. Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve a smaller instance of the same problem. It requires a base case to stop the calls and a recursive case that reduces the problem size. The intuition is that complex problems can be broken down into simpler, similar subproblems. For example, calculating factorial: n! = n * (n-1)!, with base case 0! = 1. Stacks are linear data structures that follow LIFO (last-in, first-out) order, with two main operations: push (add to top) and pop (remove from top). They can be implemented using arrays or linked lists. The connection is that recursion uses an implicit call stack to manage function calls, and in this problem, we use an explicit stack to store data. This works because both concepts involve nested operations: recursion nests function calls, and stacks nest elements. Step-by-step for recursion: 1. Define base case. 2. Define recursive case that calls function with modified input. For stacks: 1. Initialize empty stack. 2. Push elements. 3. Pop elements as needed. Time and space depend on implementation but are fundamental to algorithm design. This feeds into basic operations by providing the underlying principles. Common mistakes include infinite recursion from missing base case or stack underflow from popping empty stack. The real pattern is combining these to solve problems like sorting.

Simple pseudocode examples:
# Recursion example
def factorial(n):
    if n == 0:  # Base case
        return 1
    return n * factorial(n-1)  # Recursive case

# Stack example
stack = []
stack.push(1)  # stack = [1]
stack.push(2)  # stack = [1,2]
top = stack.pop()  # top=2, stack=[1]

examples.trace:
factorial(3) calls factorial(2)
factorial(2) calls factorial(1)
factorial(1) calls factorial(0)
factorial(0) returns 1
factorial(1) returns 1*1=1
factorial(2) returns 2*1=2
factorial(3) returns 3*2=6

Start: stack=[]
Push 5: stack=[5]
Push 3: stack=[5,3]
Pop: removes 3, stack=[5]
Push 7: stack=[5,7]

testCases.input: Recursion: sum of first 4 natural numbers

approaches.name: Approach 3: Recursive Sort with Divide and Conquer
approaches.summary: Apply a divide-and-conquer strategy by recursively splitting the stack into smaller parts, sorting each part, and then merging them back in sorted order using recursion. This is analogous to merge sort adapted for a stack.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Stack Using Recursive Divide and Conquer
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of sorting a stack using recursive divide-and-conquer approach
ladders.explanation: This approach adapts the merge sort algorithm to work with a stack data structure. The intuition is that we can recursively split the stack into smaller parts, sort each part independently, and then merge them back together in sorted order. Since we're working with a stack (LIFO structure), we need to carefully manage the recursion to preserve the stack properties. The algorithm works by: 1) Recursively dividing the stack into two halves until we reach base cases (empty or single-element stacks), 2) Sorting each half recursively, 3) Merging the two sorted halves back together. The key insight is that we can treat the stack as a linear structure that we can conceptually split, even though physically it's a LIFO structure. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to the divide-and-conquer nature, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This approach is elegant because it leverages the natural recursion of merge sort while adapting it to stack operations. Common mistakes include not handling the base cases correctly, incorrectly managing stack elements during the merge phase, and not understanding how the recursion unwinds to build the sorted stack. The algorithm must carefully push and pop elements to maintain the sorted order while respecting the LIFO nature of stacks.

examples.trace:
Initial stack: [5, 1, 4, 2, 3] (top=5)
1. Split into halves: left=[5, 1, 4], right=[2, 3]
2. Sort left half: [5, 1, 4]
   - Split: left=[5, 1], right=[4]
   - Sort [5, 1]: split to [5] and [1], both sorted, merge to [5, 1]
   - Sort [4]: already sorted
   - Merge [5, 1] and [4]: result [5, 4, 1]
3. Sort right half: [2, 3]
   - Split: [2] and [3], both sorted
   - Merge: [3, 2]
4. Merge halves [5, 4, 1] and [3, 2]:
   - Compare 5 and 3, push 5
   - Compare 4 and 3, push 4
   - Compare 1 and 3, push 3
   - Push 2, then 1
   - Result: [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]

Initial: [10, -5, 0, 8, 3, 7]
1. Split: left=[10, -5, 0], right=[8, 3, 7]
2. Sort left: [10, -5, 0]
   - Split: [10, -5] and [0]
   - Sort [10, -5]: split to [10] and [-5], merge to [10, -5]
   - Merge [10, -5] and [0]: [10, 0, -5]
3. Sort right: [8, 3, 7]
   - Split: [8, 3] and [7]
   - Sort [8, 3]: split to [8] and [3], merge to [8, 3]
   - Merge [8, 3] and [7]: [8, 7, 3]
4. Merge [10, 0, -5] and [8, 7, 3]:
   - Compare 10 and 8, push 10
   - Compare 0 and 8, push 8
   - Compare 0 and 7, push 7
   - Compare 0 and 3, push 3
   - Push 0, then -5
   - Result: [10, 8, 7, 3, 0, -5]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Sorted Stacks Recursively
ladders.desc: Merge two already-sorted stacks into one sorted stack using recursion
ladders.explanation: This is the core merging operation that combines two sorted stacks into one sorted stack. The intuition is similar to the merge step in merge sort: we compare the top elements of both stacks, take the larger one (since we want descending order from top to bottom), and recursively merge the remaining elements. The key insight is that even though stacks are LIFO, if both are sorted in descending order (largest on top), we can merge them by always taking the larger top element. The algorithm: 1) Base case: if either stack is empty, return the other stack. 2) Compare top elements of both stacks. 3) Pop the larger element and store it. 4) Recursively merge the remaining stacks. 5) Push the stored element back onto the merged result. Time complexity is O(m+n) where m and n are sizes of the two stacks. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the recursion stack. This sub-routine is critical because it's used after sorting each half - without correct merging, the divide-and-conquer approach won't work. Common mistakes include not handling empty stacks correctly, pushing elements in wrong order, and infinite recursion when stacks have equal elements. The recursion must carefully preserve elements during the unwinding phase.

examples.trace:
Merge([5,3,1], [4,2]):
1. Compare tops: 5 > 4, pop 5, store 5
2. Merge([3,1], [4,2]): compare 3 and 4, 4 > 3, pop 4, store 4
3. Merge([3,1], [2]): compare 3 and 2, 3 > 2, pop 3, store 3
4. Merge([1], [2]): compare 1 and 2, 2 > 1, pop 2, store 2
5. Merge([1], []): return [1]
6. Push stored 2: [2, 1]
7. Push stored 3: [3, 2, 1]
8. Push stored 4: [4, 3, 2, 1]
9. Push stored 5: [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]

Merge([10,7], [9,8,6]):
1. Compare 10 and 9: 10 > 9, pop 10, store 10
2. Merge([7], [9,8,6]): compare 7 and 9: 9 > 7, pop 9, store 9
3. Merge([7], [8,6]): compare 7 and 8: 8 > 7, pop 8, store 8
4. Merge([7], [6]): compare 7 and 6: 7 > 6, pop 7, store 7
5. Merge([], [6]): return [6]
6. Push 7: [7, 6]
7. Push 8: [8, 7, 6]
8. Push 9: [9, 8, 7, 6]
9. Push 10: [10, 9, 8, 7, 6]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Stack Splitting for Divide and Conquer
ladders.desc: Divide a stack into two approximately equal halves using recursion
ladders.explanation: This is the division step of the divide-and-conquer approach. The intuition is that to sort a stack using merge sort principles, we first need to split it into smaller subproblems. However, unlike arrays where we can directly access middle elements, with stacks we need to carefully pop elements to create two separate stacks. The algorithm works by recursively popping elements from the original stack and distributing them alternately or by counting to create two roughly equal stacks. The key insight is that we can use recursion itself to help with the splitting - as we pop elements, we can build two new stacks. One common approach is to pop half the elements into one stack, and the remaining into another. Time complexity is O(n) for splitting n elements. Space complexity is O(n) for the new stacks. This step is crucial because without proper splitting, the merge sort approach won't work efficiently. Common mistakes include not splitting evenly (which affects the O(n log n) complexity), not preserving the original order during splitting (which doesn't matter for sorting), and creating unnecessary temporary data structures. The split should create two stacks that can be independently sorted.

examples.trace:
Split [5,1,4,2,3] (size=5):
1. Pop 3, assign to right
2. Pop 2, assign to left
3. Pop 4, assign to right
4. Pop 1, assign to left
5. Pop 5, assign to right
Result: left has [1,2,5]? Wait need to track order...
Better approach with counting:
Target sizes: left=3, right=2
1. Pop 3 -> right (size now 1)
2. Pop 2 -> right (size now 0, right full)
3. Pop 4 -> left
4. Pop 1 -> left
5. Pop 5 -> left
Left: [5,1,4], Right: [2,3]

Split [10,-5,0,8,3,7] (size=6):
Target: left=3, right=3
1. Pop 7 -> right (2 more needed)
2. Pop 3 -> right (1 more needed)
3. Pop 8 -> right (right full)
4. Pop 0 -> left
5. Pop -5 -> left
6. Pop 10 -> left
Left: [10,-5,0], Right: [8,3,7]

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Stack Reversal
ladders.desc: Reverse a stack using recursion (foundational for merge operations)
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is crucial because when merging sorted stacks, we often need to reverse or reorder elements. The intuition is that we can use recursion to pop all elements, store them in the call stack, and then push them back in reverse order. This demonstrates how recursion can manipulate stack order. The algorithm: 1) Base case: if stack is empty, return. 2) Pop and store the top element. 3) Recursively reverse the remaining stack. 4) Push the stored element back. The key insight is that the recursion call stack itself acts as temporary storage - elements popped first get pushed last, resulting in reversal. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each element twice (pop and push). Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This building block is important because in the full solution, we might need to reverse stacks during merging or to prepare them for certain operations. Common mistakes include not understanding how the recursion unwinding reverses the order, trying to use loops (not allowed in pure recursion solutions), and not handling the base case correctly. Mastering this helps understand how recursion interacts with stack data structures.

examples.trace:
Reverse [1,2,3]:
1. Pop 1, store 1, reverse [2,3]
2. Pop 2, store 2, reverse [3]
3. Pop 3, store 3, reverse [] (base case)
4. Push 3: [3]
5. Push 2: [3,2]
6. Push 1: [3,2,1]

Reverse [5,4,3,2,1]:
1. Pop 5, store 5, reverse [4,3,2,1]
2. Pop 4, store 4, reverse [3,2,1]
3. Pop 3, store 3, reverse [2,1]
4. Pop 2, store 2, reverse [1]
5. Pop 1, store 1, reverse []
6. Push 1: [1]
7. Push 2: [1,2]
8. Push 3: [1,2,3]
9. Push 4: [1,2,3,4]
10. Push 5: [1,2,3,4,5]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Insert Element in Sorted Stack Using Recursion
ladders.desc: Insert a single element into already-sorted stack maintaining sorted order
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation demonstrates how to maintain sorted order in a stack using recursion. The intuition is that we can recursively pop elements until we find the correct position for the new element, then push everything back. For a stack sorted in descending order (largest on top), we: 1) Base case: if stack is empty OR new element is ≥ top element, push new element. 2) Otherwise, pop and store current top. 3) Recursively insert new element into remaining stack. 4) Push stored element back. The key insight is that recursion allows us to "look ahead" to find the correct insertion point without needing explicit loops. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case (inserting smallest element). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This operation is fundamental because it's a simpler version of the merge operation - instead of merging two stacks, we're merging one element into a stack. Common mistakes include incorrect comparison operators (≥ vs >), not handling the base case correctly, and not understanding how the recursion preserves order. This builds intuition for how sorted order can be maintained in a LIFO structure.

examples.trace:
Insert 4 into [5,3,1]:
1. Compare 4 with top 5: 4 < 5, so pop 5, store 5
2. Insert 4 into [3,1]: compare 4 with 3: 4 > 3, so push 4
3. Stack now [4,3,1]
4. Push stored 5: [5,4,3,1]

Insert 2 into [10,7,3]:
1. Compare 2 with 10: 2 < 10, pop 10, store 10
2. Insert 2 into [7,3]: compare 2 with 7: 2 < 7, pop 7, store 7
3. Insert 2 into [3]: compare 2 with 3: 2 < 3, pop 3, store 3
4. Insert 2 into []: empty stack, push 2
5. Push stored 3: [3,2]
6. Push stored 7: [7,3,2]
7. Push stored 10: [10,7,3,2]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Stack Recursion Basics
ladders.desc: Basic recursion on stacks: popping all elements and pushing them back
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for understanding recursive stack operations. The intuition is that recursion naturally complements stack data structures because both use the LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) principle. When a function calls itself recursively, each call creates a new stack frame, similar to pushing onto a stack. The algorithm demonstrates this symmetry: 1) Base case: if stack is empty, return. 2) Pop and store the top element. 3) Recursively process the remaining stack. 4) Push the stored element back. The key insight is that the recursion call stack mirrors the data stack operations - elements popped from the data stack are temporarily stored in the call stack. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each element. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This foundation is crucial because it shows how recursion can traverse and manipulate stacks without explicit loops. Common beginner mistakes include not understanding the base case (leading to infinite recursion), not storing popped elements correctly, and confusing the data stack with the call stack. This exercise helps build intuition for how recursion "remembers" state through the call stack, which is essential for more complex recursive stack algorithms.

examples.trace:
Process [1,2,3]:
1. Pop 1, store 1, process [2,3]
2. Pop 2, store 2, process [3]
3. Pop 3, store 3, process [] (base case)
4. Push 3: [3]
5. Push 2: [2,3]
6. Push 1: [1,2,3]
Original order restored

Process [5,4,3,2,1]:
1. Pop 5, store 5, process [4,3,2,1]
2. Pop 4, store 4, process [3,2,1]
3. Pop 3, store 3, process [2,1]
4. Pop 2, store 2, process [1]
5. Pop 1, store 1, process []
6. Push 1: [1]
7. Push 2: [2,1]
8. Push 3: [3,2,1]
9. Push 4: [4,3,2,1]
10. Push 5: [5,4,3,2,1]
